past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 10 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Benign prostatic hyperplasia, diagnosed 3 years ago
Chronic constipation, worsening over the past 4 years
Recurrent urinary tract infections over the past 2 years
Erectile dysfunction, onset approximately 3 years ago
Episode of near-syncope 6 months ago attributed to orthostatic hypotension
family_history: Father died at age 78 of myocardial infarction, no neurological disease
Mother alive at age 84, mild essential tremor diagnosed in her 60s
No known family history of Parkinson's disease, dementia, or ataxia

social_history.occupation: Civil engineer, still working full-time but colleagues have noted he is slower completing tasks
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home. Two adult children nearby
social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, quit 20 years ago (10 pack-year history)
social_history.exercise: Used to jog 3-4 times per week, has stopped over the past year due to balance concerns and lightheadedness on exertion

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, date, and situation. MMSE 28/30 (lost 1 point on serial sevens, 1 point on delayed recall). MoCA 25/30 with mild difficulty on trail-making B and phonemic fluency. Speech is mildly hypophonic but fluent and grammatically correct. No dysarthria. Affect is mildly flattened but appropriate.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light. Extraocular movements full without nystagmus. No vertical gaze palsy. Facial expression is mildly hypomimic bilaterally. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without fasciculations. No jaw jerk.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal throughout. Tone is increased bilaterally in upper and lower extremities with cogwheel rigidity at wrists, symmetric (left equals right). No clear resting tremor observed during examination; a mild irregular postural tremor is noted in both hands. Mild bilateral bradykinesia demonstrated on finger tapping, hand opening/closing, and toe tapping, symmetric in severity. No pronator drift. No myoclonus or dystonia.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. Vibration sense intact. No sensory level. Romberg positive with notable postural sway.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric in biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, and patellar bilaterally. Achilles reflexes 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No frontal release signs. No Hoffmann sign.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin testing intact. Rapid alternating movements are slow bilaterally but without cerebellar-type irregularity.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly wide-based gait with reduced arm swing bilaterally. Stride length is shortened. Turns require multiple steps. Pull test reveals marked retropulsion with inability to self-correct, requiring examiner to catch patient on 2 of 3 trials. Tandem gait impaired.
neurological_exam.additional: Orthostatic vital signs: supine BP 142/88 mmHg, HR 68; standing at 1 minute BP 108/62 mmHg, HR 72 (drop of 34/26 mmHg with inadequate heart rate compensation). Patient reports lightheadedness upon standing. No cerebellar dysarthria. No apraxia of eyelid opening. No alien limb phenomenon. No applause sign.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive slowness of movement, balance difficulties, and lightheadedness over the past 18 months
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Robert Callahan is a 60-year-old right-handed civil engineer who presents to the neurology outpatient clinic accompanied by his wife, Karen, for evaluation of progressive motor slowing, gait instability, and recurrent episodes of lightheadedness that have developed over the past eighteen months. His wife first noticed changes approximately eighteen months ago when he began having difficulty keeping pace while walking together on their evening walks, something they had done without issue for years. Over the ensuing months, she observed that his movements became generally slower and more deliberate, affecting activities such as buttoning his shirt, tying his shoes, and cutting food at dinner. He has not noticed a tremor, and his wife concurs that she has not seen a clear resting tremor, though she has observed his hands shake slightly when holding up a newspaper or a cup of coffee. Approximately twelve months ago, he fell backward while stepping off a curb and sustained a minor scalp laceration. Since then, he has had three additional falls, all involving loss of balance in the backward direction, including one episode in which he fell in the shower. His balance difficulties have made him increasingly cautious, and he now uses a handrail whenever available. Concurrently, he has experienced worsening lightheadedness, particularly upon standing from a seated or lying position, and occasionally after meals. Six months ago he nearly lost consciousness while standing in line at the grocery store and was evaluated in urgent care, where orthostatic hypotension was documented. His primary care physician reduced his lisinopril dose from 20 mg to 10 mg, but the lightheadedness has persisted. His wife also reports that he has had increasing difficulty with urinary urgency and has had two urinary tract infections in the past year, which his urologist attributed to incomplete bladder emptying. His chronic constipation, present for approximately four years, has worsened considerably despite daily polyethylene glycol, and he now sometimes goes five to six days without a bowel movement. His erectile dysfunction predates his motor symptoms by approximately one to two years. He denies visual hallucinations, vivid dreams, or acting out dreams during sleep. He reports mild difficulty concentrating at work but denies significant memory problems. His handwriting has become smaller, which he attributed to rushing. He has not been exposed to neuroleptics, metoclopramide, or other dopamine-blocking agents. He has no history of encephalitis, head trauma, or toxic exposures. His mother has a longstanding essential tremor, but there is no family history of parkinsonism or ataxia.

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Subtle linear hyperintense signal along the posterolateral margin of the bilateral putamen, more conspicuous on the left
signal_characteristics.SWI: Hypointense rim along the posterolateral putamen bilaterally, consistent with iron deposition

signal_characteristics.T1_sagittal: Pontine volume at the lower limit of normal. Cerebellar vermis and hemispheres appear mildly reduced in volume. No definite hot cross bun sign.
signal_characteristics.T2_axial: No cruciform pontine hyperintensity identified on axial T2 images

signal_characteristics.T1_sagittal: Midbrain tegmental area preserved, no obvious hummingbird sign
signal_characteristics.T2_axial: Normal midbrain signal intensity and morphology

signal_characteristics.T2_FLAIR: Scattered punctate periventricular white matter hyperintensities, age-appropriate

signal_characteristics.T1: Caudate nuclei appear normal. Ventricles are normal in size and configuration without disproportionate ventriculomegaly. No features of NPH.

volumetrics.putaminal_volume: Reduced bilaterally, below 10th percentile for age
volumetrics.cerebellar_volume: Low normal, approximately 15th percentile for age
volumetrics.pontine_volume: Low normal, approximately 12th percentile for age
volumetrics.midbrain_area: Within normal limits (130 mm2, normal >100 mm2)

additional_observations: No acute infarcts on DWI sequences
No mass lesions or pathological enhancement post-gadolinium
No evidence of basal ganglia infarcts or vascular parkinsonism
No ventriculomegaly disproportionate to sulcal atrophy
mri.impression: Mild bilateral putaminal atrophy with subtle T2/FLAIR hyperintensity along the posterolateral putaminal margins and SWI hypointensity suggesting iron deposition. Borderline low pontine and cerebellar volumes without definitive hot cross bun sign or middle cerebellar peduncle signal abnormality. Preserved midbrain morphology without hummingbird sign. These findings, while nonspecific, raise the possibility of an atypical parkinsonian syndrome, particularly multiple system atrophy (parkinsonian type, MSA-P). The putaminal rim sign, when present, has been associated with MSA-P. However, findings are subtle and clinical correlation is essential. Idiopathic Parkinson's disease and progressive supranuclear palsy remain in the differential.
recommended_actions: DaTscan to evaluate dopamine transporter integrity
Levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
Autonomic function testing to characterize autonomic failure
Clinical follow-up to monitor progression and refine differential

Additional.clinical_significance: Low supine norepinephrine level suggestive of preganglionic autonomic failure, which may favor MSA over idiopathic Parkinson's disease

labs.interpretation: Routine laboratory work-up is unremarkable including normal CBC, BMP, thyroid function, vitamin B12, RPR, and HIV screening. Ceruloplasmin and serum copper are normal, effectively excluding Wilson disease. Notably, supine serum norepinephrine is low at 98 pg/mL (reference 112-658 pg/mL), which may suggest preganglionic autonomic failure. In the context of prominent orthostatic hypotension and other autonomic symptoms, this finding raises concern for multiple system atrophy rather than idiopathic Parkinson's disease, in which norepinephrine levels are typically preserved or only mildly reduced at this stage.
abnormal_values_summary: Serum norepinephrine (supine): 98 pg/mL (low, suggestive of preganglionic autonomic failure)

signal_characteristics.pattern: Markedly reduced dopamine transporter uptake in bilateral putamina, symmetric, with relative preservation of caudate uptake

additional_observations: Bilateral symmetric putaminal dopamine transporter loss is notable; idiopathic Parkinson's disease typically shows asymmetric involvement
The degree of putaminal loss is severe relative to the clinical duration of symptoms
Caudate involvement is borderline, which may suggest a more aggressive degenerative process
output.impression: ABNORMAL DaTscan demonstrating markedly reduced bilateral putaminal dopamine transporter uptake, symmetric in distribution, with borderline caudate involvement. The symmetric pattern of dopaminergic denervation is atypical for idiopathic Parkinson's disease, which characteristically shows asymmetric putaminal involvement. This symmetric bilateral pattern is more consistent with an atypical parkinsonian syndrome such as multiple system atrophy (MSA-P) or progressive supranuclear palsy. Clinical correlation with autonomic testing and levodopa responsiveness is recommended.
recommended_actions: Levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
Clinical follow-up at 6-month intervals to monitor progression

Levodopa_Challenge.clinical_significance: Only 17.6% improvement, which is below the 30% threshold considered a significant response
Poor levodopa response, atypical for idiopathic Parkinson's disease

Levodopa_Challenge.value: Patient reports minimal improvement in ease of movement
Mild nausea, orthostatic BP drop to 96/58 mmHg at 90 minutes post-dose
Levodopa_Challenge.clinical_significance: Significant orthostatic hypotension provoked by levodopa, consistent with autonomic failure

output.interpretation: The levodopa challenge test demonstrates a poor motor response with only 17.6% improvement in UPDRS-III motor score, well below the 30% threshold considered clinically significant for supporting a diagnosis of idiopathic Parkinson's disease. The response was further complicated by significant orthostatic hypotension provoked by the levodopa dose. A poor levodopa response early in disease is a red flag for atypical parkinsonism, particularly multiple system atrophy or progressive supranuclear palsy. However, it should be noted that some patients with these conditions may show a modest initial response that wanes over time, and a chronic levodopa trial of adequate dose and duration may still be warranted.
abnormal_values_summary: Orthostatic BP drop post-levodopa: to 96/58 mmHg (significant autonomic effect)

Tilt_Table_Test.clinical_significance: Severe orthostatic hypotension with 46/30 mmHg drop, meeting criteria for neurogenic orthostatic hypotension
Inadequate heart rate compensation (only 6 bpm increase), consistent with neurogenic rather than non-neurogenic cause

Tilt_Table_Test.value: Lightheadedness, near-syncope at 4 minutes requiring return to supine position

Thermoregulatory_Sweat_Test.clinical_significance: Diffuse anhidrosis involving trunk and all four extremities, consistent with preganglionic sudomotor failure as seen in MSA

Cardiovagal_Testing.test: Heart rate variability with deep breathing

Urodynamics.clinical_significance: Elevated post-void residual consistent with neurogenic bladder with detrusor underactivity
Increased bladder capacity suggestive of detrusor hypocontractility

output.interpretation: Comprehensive autonomic testing reveals severe, widespread autonomic failure involving cardiovascular, sudomotor, and urogenital systems. The tilt table test demonstrates severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension with a 46/30 mmHg blood pressure drop and inadequate heart rate compensation. QSART and thermoregulatory sweat testing show diffuse preganglionic sudomotor failure with 62% anhidrosis. Cardiovagal function is reduced. Urodynamic testing confirms neurogenic bladder with detrusor underactivity. The severity and pattern of autonomic failure, particularly the early and prominent presentation, diffuse sudomotor involvement, and preganglionic pattern, is highly suggestive of multiple system atrophy rather than idiopathic Parkinson's disease, in which autonomic dysfunction is typically milder and appears later in the disease course.
abnormal_values_summary: Orthostatic BP drop: 46/30 mmHg (severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension)
Heart rate compensation: only 6 bpm increase (inadequate)
Thermoregulatory sweat test: 62% anhidrosis (diffuse preganglionic failure)
Cardiovagal function: reduced (HR variability 6 bpm, Valsalva ratio 1.12)

Neuropsychological_Battery.clinical_significance: Low average, borderline impaired working memory
Impaired processing speed, likely reflecting bradyphrenia associated with subcortical pathology
Mildly impaired delayed recall with improvement on recognition, suggesting retrieval deficit rather than encoding failure
Reduced phonemic fluency, consistent with frontal-subcortical dysfunction
Impaired inhibitory control and processing speed

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological profile demonstrates a frontal-subcortical pattern of cognitive impairment with reduced processing speed, impaired executive functions (set-shifting, inhibitory control, phonemic fluency), and mild retrieval-based memory deficit that improves with recognition cues. Visuospatial abilities and naming are preserved. This pattern is consistent with subcortical pathology and is seen in both Parkinson's disease and multiple system atrophy. The relatively early appearance of executive dysfunction may slightly favor MSA, though this pattern is also seen in PD with mild cognitive impairment.
abnormal_values_summary: Trail Making Test B: 118 seconds (impaired executive function)
CVLT-3 Long Delay Free Recall: z = -1.2 (mildly impaired)

Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Reduced sleep efficiency with frequent awakenings
Tonic and phasic EMG activity during REM sleep, consistent with REM sleep behavior disorder

Polysomnography.value: Two episodes of arm flailing and vocalization during REM sleep observed by technician
Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Dream enactment behavior confirming clinically manifest REM sleep behavior disorder

Polysomnography.value: Intermittent nocturnal stridor noted during NREM sleep
Polysomnography.clinical_significance: Nocturnal stridor is a characteristic and potentially dangerous feature of MSA, resulting from vocal cord abductor paralysis

output.interpretation: Polysomnography demonstrates REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD) with both electrographic evidence of REM sleep without atonia and clinically observed dream enactment behaviors. RBD is a well-established prodromal marker for alpha-synucleinopathies including Parkinson's disease, dementia with Lewy bodies, and multiple system atrophy. Critically, intermittent nocturnal stridor was observed during NREM sleep, which is a characteristic and potentially life-threatening finding in MSA due to vocal cord abductor paralysis. Nocturnal stridor in the setting of parkinsonism with autonomic failure strongly supports the diagnosis of MSA. Mild obstructive sleep apnea is also noted. The patient should be counseled regarding the significance of stridor and the potential need for CPAP or, in severe cases, tracheostomy.
abnormal_values_summary: REM sleep without atonia: Present (REM sleep behavior disorder)
Nocturnal stridor: Present during NREM sleep (characteristic of MSA)

additional_observations: Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake argues against postganglionic sympathetic denervation
In idiopathic Parkinson's disease, cardiac MIBG uptake is typically reduced due to postganglionic Lewy body pathology
Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake with severe orthostatic hypotension is characteristic of preganglionic autonomic failure as seen in MSA
output.impression: NORMAL cardiac MIBG scintigraphy with preserved heart-to-mediastinum ratios on both early and delayed images. This finding is significant in the context of severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, as it suggests preganglionic rather than postganglionic autonomic failure. In idiopathic Parkinson's disease, cardiac MIBG uptake is typically decreased due to postganglionic cardiac sympathetic denervation. Preserved MIBG uptake in a patient with parkinsonism and prominent autonomic failure strongly favors a diagnosis of multiple system atrophy over idiopathic Parkinson's disease.
recommended_actions: Correlate with autonomic function testing results
Initiate symptomatic treatment for orthostatic hypotension
Consider referral to a movement disorder center for multidisciplinary management

results.title: The Movement Disorder Society Criteria for the Diagnosis of Multiple System Atrophy
results.authors: Wenning GK, Stankovic I, Vignatelli L, et al.
results.summary: Updated MDS diagnostic criteria for MSA. Clinically established MSA requires: (1) sporadic, progressive adult-onset parkinsonism or cerebellar syndrome, (2) severe autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension with >=20/10 mmHg drop or neurogenic urinary dysfunction), and (3) at least one additional feature. Clinically probable MSA requires parkinsonism or cerebellar syndrome plus at least one clinical or MRI feature suggestive of MSA.

results.title: Distinguishing multiple system atrophy from Parkinson's disease: clinical features and progression
results.authors: Koga S, Aoki N, Uitti RJ, et al.
results.summary: Red flags for MSA vs PD include: early and severe autonomic failure, poor levodopa response, rapid progression, symmetric parkinsonism, stridor, early postural instability, and absence of typical PD features such as asymmetric resting tremor and sustained levodopa response.

output.summary: The current MDS criteria (2022) define clinically established MSA as requiring sporadic, progressive parkinsonism or cerebellar syndrome with severe autonomic failure plus supportive features. Key distinguishing features from PD include early severe autonomic failure, poor levodopa response, symmetric parkinsonism, nocturnal stridor, rapid progression, and specific MRI findings such as putaminal rim sign, hot cross bun sign, or cerebellar/pontine atrophy.

differential.key_features: Symmetric parkinsonism, early and severe autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension, urinary dysfunction, erectile dysfunction, constipation), poor levodopa response, putaminal rim sign on MRI, symmetric DaTscan, nocturnal stridor, preserved cardiac MIBG uptake, REM sleep behavior disorder

Bradykinesia, rigidity, and mild postural tremor are present; however, the symmetry, poor levodopa response, early severe autonomic failure, and preserved cardiac MIBG uptake argue strongly against this diagnosis

Early postural instability with falls is present, but patient lacks vertical gaze palsy, axial rigidity predominance, and midbrain atrophy (hummingbird sign); autonomic failure pattern more consistent with MSA
Gait difficulty and urinary symptoms are present, but MRI shows no ventriculomegaly, no magnetic gait pattern, and imaging does not support NPH

optimal_actions.action: Perform detailed neurological examination with emphasis on symmetry of motor findings, postural instability testing (pull test), and orthostatic vital signs
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric bilateral bradykinesia and rigidity, marked postural instability with retropulsion, significant orthostatic hypotension with inadequate heart rate compensation

optimal_actions.action: Order brain MRI to evaluate for structural causes of parkinsonism and specific atypical parkinsonian features
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Putaminal atrophy with rim sign, borderline pontine/cerebellar volume loss, no hummingbird sign, no NPH features

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive laboratory work-up including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, vitamin B12, ceruloplasmin, RPR
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal results; ceruloplasmin normal excluding Wilson disease; low supine norepinephrine suggesting preganglionic autonomic failure

optimal_actions.action: Request DaTscan to confirm presynaptic dopaminergic deficit and assess symmetry
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Symmetric bilateral putaminal dopamine transporter loss, atypical for idiopathic PD

optimal_actions.action: Perform levodopa challenge test to assess dopaminergic responsiveness
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Poor motor response (<30% improvement), supporting atypical parkinsonism

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive autonomic function testing (tilt table, QSART, thermoregulatory sweat test, cardiovagal testing)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Severe neurogenic orthostatic hypotension, diffuse preganglionic sudomotor failure, reduced cardiovagal function, neurogenic bladder

optimal_actions.action: Order polysomnography to evaluate for REM sleep behavior disorder and nocturnal stridor
optimal_actions.expected_finding: RBD confirmed; nocturnal stridor detected, characteristic of MSA

optimal_actions.action: Consider cardiac MIBG scintigraphy to differentiate preganglionic vs postganglionic autonomic failure
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake, supporting preganglionic failure (MSA) over postganglionic (PD)

optimal_actions.action: Initiate symptomatic management: fludrocortisone or midodrine for orthostatic hypotension, manage urinary symptoms, address constipation, consider cautious low-dose levodopa trial
Discuss diagnosis, prognosis, and advance care planning with patient and family; refer to movement disorder center and multidisciplinary team including PT, OT, speech therapy, and palliative care

critical_actions: Recognize that symmetric parkinsonism with early severe autonomic failure is a red flag for atypical parkinsonism, not idiopathic PD
Perform orthostatic vital signs to document neurogenic orthostatic hypotension
Order autonomic function testing to characterize the extent and pattern of autonomic failure
Assess levodopa responsiveness to differentiate PD from atypical parkinsonism
Order polysomnography to evaluate for nocturnal stridor, which is potentially life-threatening in MSA
Do not attribute all symptoms to medication side effects (tamsulosin, lisinopril) without further evaluation
contraindicated_actions: Prescribing high-dose levodopa without monitoring orthostatic blood pressure, as it may worsen orthostatic hypotension
Prescribing anticholinergic medications (e.g., trihexyphenidyl) which would worsen urinary retention, constipation, and cognitive function
Attributing orthostatic hypotension solely to antihypertensive medication and failing to pursue neurogenic causes
Prescribing dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole), which are poorly tolerated in MSA due to worsening orthostatic hypotension and minimal efficacy
Delaying evaluation for nocturnal stridor, which carries risk of sudden death in MSA
Performing deep brain stimulation, which is contraindicated in MSA
key_reasoning_points: The symmetric parkinsonism without a clear resting tremor is atypical for idiopathic PD, which characteristically presents with asymmetric involvement and resting tremor

Early and severe autonomic failure (orthostatic hypotension, urinary dysfunction, erectile dysfunction, constipation) preceding or concurrent with motor symptoms is the hallmark of MSA and is unusual in early PD
Poor levodopa response (<30% improvement) early in disease strongly argues against idiopathic PD
The putaminal rim sign on MRI and symmetric putaminal DAT loss on DaTscan support MSA-P
Preserved cardiac MIBG uptake differentiates the preganglionic autonomic failure of MSA from the postganglionic failure of PD
Nocturnal stridor detected on polysomnography is a characteristic and potentially fatal feature of MSA that requires urgent management
REM sleep behavior disorder, while present in both PD and MSA, combined with the other features supports the alpha-synucleinopathy spectrum
The mother's essential tremor is a red herring that might initially bias toward a genetic tremor-predominant condition but is irrelevant to this presentation

Tamsulosin use could potentially confound assessment of orthostatic hypotension and urinary symptoms, requiring careful clinical evaluation to distinguish medication effects from neurogenic autonomic failure

metadata.author: NeuroBench generation system
metadata.clinical_notes: Diagnostic puzzle case presenting as symmetric parkinsonism with prominent autonomic failure, initially mimicking idiopathic Parkinson's disease. Key challenges include: symmetric rather than asymmetric motor findings, prominent early autonomic failure that could be attributed to medications or comorbidities, no clear resting tremor, poor levodopa response, and a family history of essential tremor that may initially mislead. The final diagnosis of MSA-P is supported by multimodal evidence including MRI putaminal rim sign, symmetric DaTscan, poor levodopa response, severe autonomic testing abnormalities, preserved cardiac MIBG, and nocturnal stridor on PSG.
teaching_points: Symmetric parkinsonism without resting tremor should prompt consideration of atypical parkinsonian syndromes
Early and severe autonomic failure is a red flag for MSA and should not be attributed solely to medications
Poor levodopa response distinguishes MSA from idiopathic PD
Cardiac MIBG scintigraphy can differentiate preganglionic (MSA) from postganglionic (PD) autonomic failure
Nocturnal stridor in MSA is potentially life-threatening and must be actively sought on polysomnography
